subject: Physics
q: In the potentiometer, when the cell in the secondary circuit is shunted with $4 \Omega$ resistance, the balance is obtained at the length 120 cm of wire. Now when the same cell is shunted with $12 \Omega$ resistance, the balance is shifted to a length of 180 cm. The internal resistance of cell is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
question: In the potentiometer, when the cell in the secondary circuit is shunted with $4 \Omega$ resistance, the balance is obtained at the length 120 cm of wire. Now when the same cell is shunted with $12 \Omega$ resistance, the balance is shifted to a length of 180 cm. The internal resistance of cell is $\_\_\_\_$ $\Omega$
solution:
In a potentiometer, the internal resistance $r$ of a cell is given by the formula $r = R \left( \frac{l_0}{l} - 1 \right)$, where $l_0$ is the balancing length in open circuit, $l$ is the balancing length when shunted with resistance $R$.
This can be rewritten as $\frac{l_0}{l} = 1 + \frac{r}{R}$ or $l = \frac{l_0}{1 + r/R}$.
Case 1: $R_1 = 4 \Omega$ and $l_1 = 120$ cm.
$120 = \frac{l_0}{1 + r/4} = \frac{4l_0}{4 + r}$ ... (i)
Case 2: $R_2 = 12 \Omega$ and $l_2 = 180$ cm.
$180 = \frac{l_0}{1 + r/12} = \frac{12l_0}{12 + r}$ ... (ii)
Dividing equation (ii) by (i):
$\frac{180}{120} = \frac{12l_0}{12 + r} \times \frac{4 + r}{4l_0}$
$\frac{3}{2} = \frac{3(4 + r)}{12 + r}$
$\frac{1}{2} = \frac{4 + r}{12 + r}$
$12 + r = 8 + 2r$
$r = 4 \Omega$
The internal resistance of the cell is $4 \Omega$.

subject: Physics
q: A carbon resistance has a following colour code. What is the value of the resistance?
question: A carbon resistance has a following colour code. What is the value of the resistance?
solution:
Colour band A and B indicate the first two significant figures of resistance in "ohm" , while the C band gives the decimal multiplier i.e., the number of zeros that follows the two significant figures A and B.

The last band D indicates the tolerance in percentage about the indicate value or in another word, it represents the percentage accuracy of the indicated value. 

 

According to given color-coding, the values of the color of rings are 

Green 5
Orange 3
Yellow 4
Gold 5%
   


Value of resistance =53×104Ω

=530 kΩ

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> resistors each of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math> can be connected in such as to get maximum and minimum equivalent resistance. The ratio of maximum and minimum equivalent resistance will be________.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> resistors each of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mo>Ω</mo></math> can be connected in such as to get maximum and minimum equivalent resistance. The ratio of maximum and minimum equivalent resistance will be________.
solution:
When all the resistors are connected in series, the total resistance is

Rmax=10R

⇒Rmax=10×10=100 Ω

When all the resistors are connected in parallel, the total resistance is

 Rmin=R10

⇒Rmin=1010=1 Ω

Hence, the ratio,

 RmaxRmin=100 Ω1 Ω=100.

subject: Physics
q: When a resistance of 5 Ω is shunted with a moving coil galvanometer, it shows a full scale deflection for a current of 250 mA, however when 1050 Ω resistance is connected with it in series, it gives full scale deflection for 25 volt. The resistance of galvanometer is_________Ω.
question: When a resistance of 5 Ω is shunted with a moving coil galvanometer, it shows a full scale deflection for a current of 250 mA, however when 1050 Ω resistance is connected with it in series, it gives full scale deflection for 25 volt. The resistance of galvanometer is_________Ω.
solution:
The formula to calculate the maximum current through the galvanometer, when a shunt resistance r1 is connected is given byiGmax=r1r1+RGimax   ...1

When a series resistance r2 is connected, the potential difference across the galvanometer is given by

V=iGmaxRG+r2   ...2

From equations (1) and (2), it can be written that

V=r1r1+RGimaxRG+r2⇒Vr1+VRG=imaxr1RG+imaxr1r2⇒V-imaxr1RG=imaxr1r2-Vr1⇒RG=imaxr1r2-Vr1V-imaxr1   ...3

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (3) to calculate the required galvanometer resistance.

RG=0.250 A×5 Ω×1050 Ω-25 V×5 Ω25 V-0.250 A×5 Ω=50 Ω

subject: Physics
q: As shown in the figure, a network of resistors is connected to a battery of 24 V with an internal resistance of 3 Ω. The currents through the resistors R4 and R5 are I4 and I5 respectively. The values of I4 and I5 are:
question: As shown in the figure, a network of resistors is connected to a battery of 24 V with an internal resistance of 3 Ω. The currents through the resistors R4 and R5 are I4 and I5 respectively. The values of I4 and I5 are:
solution:
Equivalent resistance of circuit
Req=3+2×22+2+2+20×520+5+2=12 Ω

Current through battery i=2412=2 A

Now, I4=R5R4+R5×2=520+5×2=25 A

and I5=i-I4=2-25=85 A

subject: Physics
q: In a metre bridge experiment null point is obtained at $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from one end of the wire when resistance $\mathrm{X}$ is balanced against another resistance $\mathrm{Y}$. If $\mathrm{X} < \mathrm{Y}$, then the new position of the null point from the same end, if one decides to balance a resistance of $3 \mathrm{X}$ against $\mathrm{Y}$, will be close to :
question: In a metre bridge experiment null point is obtained at $40 \mathrm{~cm}$ from one end of the wire when resistance $\mathrm{X}$ is balanced against another resistance $\mathrm{Y}$. If $\mathrm{X} < \mathrm{Y}$, then the new position of the null point from the same end, if one decides to balance a resistance of $3 \mathrm{X}$ against $\mathrm{Y}$, will be close to :
solution:
From question, $\frac{x}{y}=\frac{40}{100-40}=\frac{2}{3}$
$$
\Rightarrow \mathrm{x}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{y}
$$
Again, $\frac{3 x}{y}=\frac{Z}{100-Z}$
or $\frac{3 \times \frac{2 y}{3}}{y}=\frac{Z}{100-Z}$
Solving we get $\mathrm{Z}=67 \mathrm{~cm}$
Therefore new position of null point
$$
\cong 67 \mathrm{~cm}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The supply voltage to a room is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mo>V</mo></math>. The resistance of the lead wires is <math> <mrow> <mn>6</mn> <mi> Ω</mi> </mrow> </math>. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mo>W</mo></math> bulb is already switched on. What is the decrease of voltage across the bulb, when a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mo>W</mo></math> heater is switched on in parallel to the bulb?
question: The supply voltage to a room is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>120</mn><mo> </mo><mo>V</mo></math>. The resistance of the lead wires is <math> <mrow> <mn>6</mn> <mi> Ω</mi> </mrow> </math>. A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mo>W</mo></math> bulb is already switched on. What is the decrease of voltage across the bulb, when a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>240</mn><mo> </mo><mo>W</mo></math> heater is switched on in parallel to the bulb?
solution:
Resistance of bulb, Rb=V2Pb=120×12060=240 Ω
Resistance of heater, Rh=V2Ph=120×120240=60 Ω

Voltage across bulb before the heater is not connected, V1=V×RbRb+6=120×240 V246=117.07 V

Now the bulb and heater are connected in parallel.

Resistance of the combination is Rnet=240×60240+60=48 Ω
Voltage across bulb after the heater is switched on,
V2=V×RnetRnet+6=120×4848+6=106.66 V

The decrease in the voltage is V1-V2=117.07-106.66≃10.4 V

Note: Here supply voltage is taken as rated voltage.

subject: Physics
q: In the following circuit, the battery has an emf of 2 V and an internal resistance of 23 Ω. The power consumption in the entire circuit is ______ W.
question: In the following circuit, the battery has an emf of 2 V and an internal resistance of 23 Ω. The power consumption in the entire circuit is ______ W.
solution:
As we can see, two resistances of 2 Ω in the middle portion(in both left and right side) are in parallel, so their combined resistance will be 2×22+2+2×22+2=2 Ω. Now this resistance along with the topmost & bottommost resistance are in parallel again, so total resistance of the circuit will be,

Req=23+23=43 Ω

∴P=V2Req=443=3 W

subject: Physics
q: A galvanometer of resistance <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> has <math><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi></math> divisions on its scale and has sensitivity of <math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> . It is to be converted to voltmeter with three ranges, of <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The appropriate circuit to do so is:
question: A galvanometer of resistance <math><mn>100</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">Ω</mi></math> has <math><mn>50</mn><mi> </mi></math> divisions on its scale and has sensitivity of <math><mn>20</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">μ</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi><mo>/</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">v</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">i</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">n</mi></math> . It is to be converted to voltmeter with three ranges, of <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi></math> and <math><mn>0</mn><mo>-</mo><mn>20</mn><mi> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The appropriate circuit to do so is:
solution:
Given, the resistance of the galvanometer G=100 Ω
No. of divisions =50
Current Sensitivity  =20 μA/division

The maximum current through galvanometer will be 
Imax=50×20×10-6=10-3 A
For V1 = 2 V

using Ohm's law V1= Imax G+R1
2=10-3 ×100+R1
∴R1=1900 Ω

For V2 = 10 V

using Ohm's law V2 =ImaxG+R1+R2

10=10-3 ×100 + 1900+R2
∴R2=8000 Ω

For V3 =20 V

using Ohm's law V3 =Imax G+R1+R2+R3
20=10-3 100 +1900+8000+R3
∴R3=10×103 Ω

subject: Physics
q: Two resistors of $100 \Omega$ each are connected in series with a 9 V battery. A voltmeter of $400 \Omega$ resistance is connected to measure the voltage drop across one of the resistors. The voltmeter reading is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
question: Two resistors of $100 \Omega$ each are connected in series with a 9 V battery. A voltmeter of $400 \Omega$ resistance is connected to measure the voltage drop across one of the resistors. The voltmeter reading is $\_\_\_\_$ V.
solution:
When the 400Ω voltmeter is connected in parallel with one 100Ω resistor, the equivalent resistance is
$R_{eq} = \frac{100 \times 400}{500} = 80Ω$.
Total circuit resistance: $R_{total} = 100 + 80 = 180Ω$.
Circuit current: $I = 9/180 = 0.05$ A.
Voltmeter reading (voltage across parallel combination): $V = 0.05 \times 80 = 4$ V

q:
In the given figure, a battery of emf E is connected across a conductor PQ of length l and different area of cross-sections having radii r1 and r2r2<r1.

Choose the correct option as one moves from P to Q.
question:
In the given figure, a battery of emf E is connected across a conductor PQ of length l and different area of cross-sections having radii r1 and r2r2<r1.

Choose the correct option as one moves from P to Q.
solution:
Current is constant in conductor
i=constant
Resistance of element dR=ρdxπr2
dV=idR= ipdx πr2
E=dVdx=iρπr2
and Vd=eEτm
∴ Vd×E

→E∝1r2

If r decreases, E will increase.

∴ Vd will increase.

subject: Physics
q: Two wires $A$ and $B$ are made up of the same material and have the same mass. Wire $A$ has radius of $2.0 \mathrm{~mm}$ and wire $B$ has radius of $4.0 \mathrm{~mm}$. The resistance of wire $B$ is $2 \Omega$. The resistance of wire $A$ is _____$\Omega$.
question: Two wires $A$ and $B$ are made up of the same material and have the same mass. Wire $A$ has radius of $2.0 \mathrm{~mm}$ and wire $B$ has radius of $4.0 \mathrm{~mm}$. The resistance of wire $B$ is $2 \Omega$. The resistance of wire $A$ is _____$\Omega$.
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \because \mathrm{R}=\frac{\rho \ell}{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{V}}{\mathrm{A}^2} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}=\frac{\mathrm{A}_{\mathrm{B}}^2}{\mathrm{~A}_{\mathrm{A}}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{B}}^4}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{A}}^4} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}}{2}=\left[\frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 10^{-3}}\right]^4 \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{A}}=32 \Omega .\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The battery of a mobile phone is rated as 4.2 V , 5800 mAh. How much energy is stored in it when fully charged ?
question: The battery of a mobile phone is rated as 4.2 V , 5800 mAh. How much energy is stored in it when fully charged ?
solution:
Given $\mathrm{V}=4.2$ volt
$\therefore$ Energy supplied by battery
$=\mathrm{vq}=4.2 \times 5800 \times 3600 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~J}=87.696 \mathrm{~kJ}$
$\therefore$ Energy stored in the battery when fully charged
$=87.696 \mathrm{~kJ} \approx 87.7 \mathrm{~kJ}$

subject: Physics
q: A wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>$R_{1}$ is drawn out so that its length is increased by twice of its original length. The ratio of new resistance to original resistance is:
question: A wire of resistance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>$R_{1}$ is drawn out so that its length is increased by twice of its original length. The ratio of new resistance to original resistance is:
solution:
As per the given question, the change in the length is ∆l=2l.

Resistance of a wire is given by, R=ρlA

Therefore, R1=ρl1 A1

When the length is starched by 2l1, final length will be l2=l1+2l1=3l1. As the volume remains constant l2A2=l1A1  ⇒A2=A13. Therefore,

R2=ρ3l1 A13=9ρl1 A1

∴R2R1=9

subject: Physics

subject: Physics
q: Two cells are connected in opposition as shown. Cell E1 is of 8 V emf and 2 Ω internal resistance; the cell E2 is of 2 V emf and 4 Ω internal resistance. The terminal potential difference of cell E2 is ______ V.
question: Two cells are connected in opposition as shown. Cell E1 is of 8 V emf and 2 Ω internal resistance; the cell E2 is of 2 V emf and 4 Ω internal resistance. The terminal potential difference of cell E2 is ______ V.
solution:
Current in circuit, I=8-22+4=66=1 A

Applying Kirchhoff's law from C to B,

VC-2-4×1=VB

⇒VC-VB=6 V

subject: Physics
q: Mobility of electrons in a semiconductor is defined as the ratio of their drift velocity to the applied electric field. If, for an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor, the density of electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>19</mn></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and their mobility is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.6</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the resistivity of the semiconductor (since it is an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor contribution of holes is ignored) is close to:
question: Mobility of electrons in a semiconductor is defined as the ratio of their drift velocity to the applied electric field. If, for an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor, the density of electrons is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>19</mn></msup><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup></math> and their mobility is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.6</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the resistivity of the semiconductor (since it is an <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mtext>N</mtext></math>-type semiconductor contribution of holes is ignored) is close to:
solution:
Resistivity ρ=EJ=Eneυd=1neμ

μ= mobility

ρ=11019×1.6×10-19×1.6=12.56 Ω m

=0.4 Ω m

subject: Physics
q: Two batteries with e.m.f. 12 V and 13 V are connected in parallel across a load resistor of 10 Ω. The internal resistance of the two batteries are 1 Ω and 2 Ω respectively. The voltage across the load lies between,
question: Two batteries with e.m.f. 12 V and 13 V are connected in parallel across a load resistor of 10 Ω. The internal resistance of the two batteries are 1 Ω and 2 Ω respectively. The voltage across the load lies between,
solution:
As shown in figure two cells are connected in parallel with the load resistance of R=10 Ω. Let current i1, i2 drawn from both cells as shown, from K.C.L.  i = i1+ i2  ...1

Apply K.V.L. for loop ACDF, 

E1-i R-i1 r1=0⇒12-10i-i1=0  ...2

From equations 1 and 2

12-11 i1-10i2=0  ...3

Now, for loop BCDE, 

Apply K.V.L.

E2-10i-i2r2=0⇒13-10 i-2i2=0⇒∵i=i1+i2∴ 13-10 i1-12 i2=0  ...4

From equation 3 and 4, 

i1=716 A, i2=2332 A

So, net current in external load R=10 Ω is

I=i1+i2=716+2332=3732 A

Then potential difference for a given load, 

V=IR=3732×32=11·56 V

So, the potential difference lies between, 11·5 V and 11·6 V.

subject: Physics
q: Two sources of equal emf are connected to an external resistance R. The internal resistance of the two sources are $R_1$ and $R_2\left(R_2>R_1\right)$. If the potential difference across the source having internal resistance $\mathrm{R}_2$ is zero, then
question: Two sources of equal emf are connected to an external resistance R. The internal resistance of the two sources are $R_1$ and $R_2\left(R_2>R_1\right)$. If the potential difference across the source having internal resistance $\mathrm{R}_2$ is zero, then
solution:
$\mathrm{I}=\frac{2 \mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2+\mathrm{R}}$
$\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{R}_2 \mathrm{I}=0$
$\Rightarrow \mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_2-\mathrm{R}_1$
